coredata.dc:title: DES-driven sustainable dual valorization of lignocellulose into carbon dots and porous biochar for effective wastewater remediation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Hong, Si
Wei, Aocheng
Xie, Chao
Shen, Xiaojun
Wen, Jia-Long
Yuan, Tong-Qi
coredata.dc:description: Deep eutectic solvents (DES) are renowned for their effectiveness in deconstructing lignocellulose and extracting lignin, yet the challenges lie in lignin condensation and the disposal of the DES remnants after pretreatment. To overcome these issues, this work proposed a holistic strategy utilizing deep eutectic solvent (DES)-driven lignocellulose deconstruction to upgrade lignocellulose into nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDs) and iron-decorated porous carbons, serving as photocatalysts and adsorbents, respectively. These nitrogen-doped CDs via the choline chloride/FeCl3 DES pretreatment exhibited abundant nitrogen/oxygen functional groups, enhancing photocatalytic activities and facilitating effective charge separation and transfer. The photocatalytic efficiency of the CDs on dyes reached 97 % under acidic conditions primarily, and free radical quenching experiments indicated that singlet oxygen was the dominant oxidant species. Moreover, the adsorption capabilities of Fe-decorated porous carbons for Congo red reached 2432.3 mg·g−1, surpassing most existing carbon materials. The adsorption mechanism was due to a synergistic effect including physical adsorption, coordination, hydrogen bonding, electrostatic, and π-π interactions. This study proposed a synergetic conversion of DES and lignocellulose into functional carbon materials for wastewater remediation, which inspired the development of a green and cost-effective biorefinery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lignocellulose
Deep eutectic solvent
Carbon dots
Photocatalysis
Dye removal
Sustainable biorefinery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Property and photodegradation performance of carbon dots
Structural characterization and adsorption ability of Fe-doped porous carbon

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (200-BLX202234), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31971613 and 22308029), and the 5\u00B75 Engineering Research & Innovation Team Project of Beijing Forestry University (No. BLRC 2023B05). Additionally, the authors appreciate the assistance of the Innovation Plat-form for High-Value Utilization of Forest Resources at Beijing Forestry University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: DES-driven lignocellulose deconstruction and functionalization. (a) schematic illustration of eucalyptus fractionation and building blocks upgrading into carbon dots and activated carbon, (b) yield and chemical components of lignocellulose and residues, (c) degraded product content. Note: LA, lactic acid; FA, formic acid; AA, acetic acid; LV, levulinic acid; Gul, glucose; Xyl, xylose.
Morphology and structure characteristics of carbon dots. (a) SEM images and (b) N1s high-resolution XPS spectra of carbon dots.

Properties and photodegradation performance of CDs. (a) electrochemical impedance spectra of CDs, (b) photocurrent response of CDs, (c) UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectrum of Fe-CDs, (d) comparing the MO photocatalytic capacity of Fe-CDs/PDS system and pure PDS, (e) effect of dye concentrations on the MO photocatalytic performance, (f) effect of initial pH value on the MO degradation, (g) radical quenching tests of Fe-CDs, (h) bandgap alignment of Fe-CDs, (i) schematic illustration of MO photodegradation by Fe-CDs. Note: Ar, argon; TBA, tertiary butanol; EtOH, ethanol; L-His, L-histidine; VB, valence band; CB, conduction band.

Morphology and structural changes of carbon materials. (a) SEM images and the corresponding elemental mapping analyses of AC, (b) C1s high-resolution XPS spectra of AC, (c) O1s high-resolution XPS spectra of AC, (d) N1s high-resolution XPS spectra of AC, (e) Fe 2p high-resolution XPS spectra of AC, (f) nitrogen sorption isotherms, (g) Raman spectrum.

Adsorption capacity and mechanism of carbon materials to dyes. (a) adsorption efficiency of the AC to the CR dye, (b) isothermal adsorption models, (c) kinetic adsorption models, (d) adsorption capacity of the AC to different dyes at 100 mg·L−1, (e) recyclability of the AC for the adsorption of the CR dye at 100 mg·L−1, (f) comparison of the adsorption properties of the AC and other carbons to the CR dye [33,39–42], (g) comparison of adsorption amount of the CR dye on the AC with other carbons reported in the literature [33,35–45], (h) adsorption mechanism of the AC to the CR dye.

head.ce:title: DES-driven sustainable dual valorization of lignocellulose into carbon dots and porous biochar for effective wastewater remediation

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National & Local Joint Engineering Research Center on Biomass Resource Utilization
College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of Efficient Production of Forest Resources, Beijing Forestry University, Beijing 100083, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Efficient Production of Forest Resources

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Deep eutectic solvents (DES) are renowned for their effectiveness in deconstructing lignocellulose and extracting lignin, yet the challenges lie in lignin condensation and the disposal of the DES remnants after pretreatment. To overcome these issues, this work proposed a holistic strategy utilizing deep eutectic solvent (DES)-driven lignocellulose deconstruction to upgrade lignocellulose into nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDs) and iron-decorated porous carbons, serving as photocatalysts and adsorbents, respectively. These nitrogen-doped CDs via the choline chloride/FeCl3 DES pretreatment exhibited abundant nitrogen/oxygen functional groups, enhancing photocatalytic activities and facilitating effective charge separation and transfer. The photocatalytic efficiency of the CDs on dyes reached 97 % under acidic conditions primarily, and free radical quenching experiments indicated that singlet oxygen was the dominant oxidant species. Moreover, the adsorption capabilities of Fe-decorated porous carbons for Congo red reached 2432.3 mg·g−1, surpassing most existing carbon materials. The adsorption mechanism was due to a synergistic effect including physical adsorption, coordination, hydrogen bonding, electrostatic, and π-π interactions. This study proposed a synergetic conversion of DES and lignocellulose into functional carbon materials for wastewater remediation, which inspired the development of a green and cost-effective biorefinery.

Completely upgrading lignocellulose into carbon dots and porous carbons by deep eutectic solvent-driven lignocellulose deconstruction for wastewater remediation.
Unlabelled Image

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In view of the continuing demand for fuels and materials in human activities, the development of renewable feedstocks and sustainable technologies is essential to reduce dependence on fossil resources radically. Abundant and renewable lignocellulose, which consists of cellulose, hemicelluloses, and lignin, is an enormous potential alternative to nonrenewable resources for the preparation of bio-based fuels and materials [1]. However, the highly cross-linked structure and complex compositions of lignocellulose largely hamper its utilization efficiency. The deconstruction and fractionation of lignocellulose are of great significance in the effective breakage of its natural recalcitrant structure and the complete utilization of its major compositions, avoiding the waste of resources. Recently, deep eutectic solvent (DES) has been applied in the field of lignocellulose processing due to its excellent performance in selectively dissolved lignin and increasing glucose yield [2–4].

Currently, using DES to separate high-yield and valuable lignin from lignocellulose has drawn increasing attention, with a variety of DES systems being designed and applied for the deconstruction and fractionation of lignocellulose [5]. Typically, the acidic DES pretreatment can result in >95% delignification and almost all hemicelluloses removal, promoting the lignocellulose fractionation [2,6]. Nevertheless, the structural integrity of the acidic DES-extracted lignin is normally significantly altered, accompanied by the cleavage of ether linkages [7,8]. To extract more lignin and simultaneously reserve more ether linkages, the polyol-based acidic DES is excessively used to extract low-condensation lignin fragments [9–11]. However, the use of acidic DES inevitably resulted in the occurrence of a part condensation reaction within lignin, thereby impeding the effective conversion of the recovered lignin into valuable chemicals and materials. Consequently, rather than attempting to preserve ether linkages in the lignin during DES pretreatment for subsequent depolymerization into small molecules, it is preferable to apply acidic DES directly to get highly condensed lignin fragments. These fragments, characterized by their rich aromatic ring structures and extensive condensation, are ideal precursors for the synthesis of advanced carbon materials. Moreover, the DES lignin-derived carbon materials exhibited notable advantages including high carbon content, various heteroatoms, and functional groups, which are suitable as adsorbents for the efficient removal of dye molecules from wastewater.

Despite the efficiency of DES pretreatment in isolating lignin from biomass, a notable challenge remains the separation of hemicelluloses-derived saccharides and organic acids and lignin-derived phenolic small molecules from the DES mixture [7,8,12]. This issue complicates the subsequent recycling and reuse of DES. Due to DES's substantial potential as a dopant and template, utilizing DES (especially metal-based DES) in the fabrication of functional carbon materials from renewable lignocellulose via the solvothermal process has sparked considerable interest [13–15]. After the separation of the DES-pretreated solid residues, the recovered DES containing the degradation products from carbohydrate and lignin can directly be used as carbon resources and dopants to prepare heteroatoms-doped carbon dots (CDs) by the traditional hydrothermal process [12,16,17]. Currently, CDs are extensively used as photocatalysts for the photocatalytic degradation of pollutants due to their tunable photoluminescence, long-term photostability, and low toxicity [18]. However, most contaminant-treated materials depend on fossil resources, which is not in line with green and sustainable development [19,20]. Therefore, deconstructing lignocellulose by metal-based DES and the direct conversion of various components into functional carbon materials for wastewater remediation can push forward the holistic lignocellulose conversion and alleviate water contamination.

Herein, a sustainable and holistic strategy, the DES-driven lignocellulose deconstruction and the preparation of functional carbon materials, were specifically designed for efficient wastewater remediation. This work synthesized a novel metal-based DES (ChCl/ferric chloride (FeCl3)) for the deconstruction of lignocellulose and the generation of highly condensed lignin. The recovered DES mixture, rich in solubilized degraded lignocellulose fragments, was adequately converted into nitrogen-doped CDs as photocatalysts for dye removal in the effluents. The remaining solid residues retained condensed lignin fragments and some leftover DES, which was a promising carbon precursor for the preparation of heteroatoms-doped porous carbons. The well-defined structure of these functional carbon materials was dissected to seize insights into why these carbon materials can efficiently remove dyes. This dual valorization approach not only converts lignocellulose into high-value carbon materials and achieves the sustainable utilization of DES-derived waste, but also establishes a novel paradigm that harmonizes sustainable biorefinery with environmental remediation, thereby marking a significant stride towards advancing the applications of green chemistry.

Eucalyptus grandis × E. urophylla was obtained from Guangxi, China. ChCl (98 %), FeCl3 (98 %), aluminum chloride (AlCl3, ≥98 %), cupric chloride (CuCl2, 98 %), potassium persulfate (PDS, 99 %), potassium hydroxide (85 %), tertiary butanol (TBA, ≥99 %), vital red (VR, AR), and amido black (AB, AR) were supplied by Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethanol (99 %), Congo red (CR, 98 %), methylene blue (MB, >98 %), methyl orange (MO, 96 %), crystal violet (CV, 99 %), and direct red 80 (DR, AR) were provided by Mareda Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hydrochloric acid (AR) was supplied by Modern Oriental Technology Development Co., LTD (Beijing, China). L-histidine (99 %)was supplied by Tianjin Xiensi Opod Technology Co., Ltd.

DES pretreatment was carried out by mixing 1.0 g eucalyptus and 10.0 g ChCl/FeCl3 in reaction vessels. The DES pretreatment was performed at 120 °C for 6 h. After the pretreatment, the mixture was poured into 30 mL of deionized water under stirring for 0.5 h. The residual solid fractions and DES soluble fractions were obtained by the filtration. The residual solid fraction was freeze-dried and subsequently used as a carbon precursor.

The recovered DES fraction was placed in a reactive vessel and treated at 200 °C for 8 h. After the reaction, the reaction solution was filtered to acquire the liquid fraction. Then, the liquid fraction was dialyzed with the molecular weight cut-off 1000 Da and freeze-dried to obtain carbon dots labeled as Fe-CDs. The Al-CDs and Cu-CDs were synthesized using the same method using ChCl/AlCl3 and ChCl/CuCl2 pretreated liquid, respectively.

As the controls, 10.0 g ChCl and ChCl/FeCl3 were mixed with 30.0 mL deionized water and kept at 200 °C for 8 h, respectively. The reaction soluble fractions were filtrated and dialyzed with the molecular weight cut-off 1000 Da to obtain carbon dots labeled as the Ch-CDs and pure Fe-CDs.

Using the solid residues after ChCl/FeCl3 pretreatment as raw material, the porous carbon was prepared using the following procedure: 5.0 g solid fraction was evenly mixed with 5.0 g potassium hydroxide and 5.0 g deionized water. The mixture was then oven-dried at 60 °C for 6 h to remove moisture. Subsequently, the sample was carbonization with a quartz tubular furnace in a constant nitrogen atmosphere and kept at 800 °C for 6 h. After cooling, the sample was washed with 0.01 mol hydrochloric acid solution and deionized water until the filtrate was neutral and then over-dried at 105 °C for 6 h to obtain the activated carbon (AC).

In the photodegradation experiment, the CDs and PDS were added to the MO solution, which was irradiated under 455 nm ultraviolet light to photodegrade the MO dye. Before irradiation, the suspension was stirred under dark conditions for 30 min to realize an adsorption-desorption equilibrium. After filtration, the MO concentration was measured on an ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer with a wavelength of 463 nm. The MO photodegradation experiment was conducted at different dye concentrations and pH values. The following equations calculated the removal rate of the MO dye:
Removal rate of the
MO
dye
%
=
C 0
−
C t
C 0
∗ 100
C0 and Ct represent the concentrations of MO solution at the initial and t time (mg·L−1), respectively.

Active species trapping experiments were investigated by adding diverse scavengers before the reaction. 0.5 mM EtOH was used to shield hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and sulfate radicals (SO4 •−). 0.5 mM TBA was used to shield •OH, while argon atmosphere and 5 mM L-His were used to shield singlet oxygen (1O2) and superoxide radicals (•O− 2), respectively. All experiments were repeated twice under the same conditions to determine the standard deviations, and the results were expressed as the average values.

10 mg of the activated carbon was mixed with 100 mL of the corresponding dye solution and continuously stirred at room temperature for 24 h. The stirring time guaranteed the equilibrium of adsorption. After filtration, the concentration of the supernatant at the maximum absorption wavelength of the tested dye was determined by an ultraviolet spectrophotometer (TU-1901, China). The adsorption capacity (Qe, mg·g−1) at the equilibrium of the tested dyes is calculated as follows:
Q e
=
C o
−
C e
∗ V
m
where C0 and Ce are the initial and equilibrium concentrations of dye solutions (mg·L−1), respectively. V is the volume of the dye solution (L) and m is the mass of the activated carbon (g).

DES pretreatment is usually used to destroy lignocellulose recalcitrance and dissociate its main components (carbohydrates and lignin) to prepare chemicals and materials. However, the condensation of lignin and degradation of hemicelluloses is unavoidable during DES pretreatment. The difficult recycling of the solubilized degradation lignocellulose components and the inefficient reusability of DES impede the industrialization of the process. Herein, a total components utilization strategy with an initial DES pretreatment was proposed for the overall upgrading of eucalyptus into CDs and porous carbon as photocatalysts and adsorbents for dye removal, respectively (Fig. 1a). The DESs with efficient lignocellulose fractionation and promoting lignin condensation were prepared using ChCl as HBA and inorganic transition metals (FeCl3, AlCl3, CuCl2) as HBD. Note that the DES pretreated mixture was added directly into deionized water and separated to obtain the residue and filtrate. Subsequently, the DES filtrate rich in the solubilized degraded lignocellulose was directly used to fabricate N-doped CDs through DES solvothermal treatment. The CDs with abundant nitrogen and oxygen groups were used as the PDS activator for the photocatalytic degradation of MO solution. Meanwhile, the remaining solid residues, containing condensed lignin fragments and some leftover DES, were carbonized to prepare N/Fe-doped porous carbons with a high specific surface area, which was suitable as an adsorbent for the adsorption of dyes. By the DES-driven lignocellulose deconstruction, this work opened a window in the value-added transformation of lignocellulosic components into dye-removed functional carbon materials, inspiring biorefinery towards “waste-treat-pollutant”.

The pretreatment effect of DES was evaluated by measuring the residual yield, chemical compositions, and degraded products in the DES liquid. As shown in Fig. 1b, the yield of residues after DES pretreatment was about 40 wt%. Chemical composition analysis showed that the DES-pretreated residues consisted of lignin and a few celluloses without hemicelluloses, indicating that the hemicelluloses and cellulose were solubilized and degraded in the DES filtrate during strongly acidic DES pretreatment. It should be noted that the residue contained a higher lignin content than that in eucalyptus, indicating that some metal compounds in the DES solution adhered to the surface of the residues. Subsequently, the concentration and yield of the degraded monomers in the DES filtrate were determined (Fig. 1c). It was found that the DES filtrate contained abundant acidic compounds such as lactic acid, formic acid, acetic acid, and levulinic acid. These acidic compounds can not only act as a carbon source but also provide an acidic environment for the synthesis of CDs. The total monomer content of ChCl/AlCl3 and ChCl/FeCl3 filtrates reached about 3.5 %, while the total monomer content of ChCl/CuCl2 filtrate was only 1.0 %. The main degraded monomers from the ChCl/AlCl3 pretreatment were glucose (1.09 g·L−1) and levulinic acid (0.50 g·L−1). The main degraded monomers from the ChCl/FeCl3 pretreatment were acetic acid (1.59 g·L−1), together with minor amounts of levulinic acid, xylose, formic acid, and lactic acid. Additionally, the pH data of DES suggested that ChCl/FeCl3 was the most acidic, followed by ChCl/AlCl3 and ChCl/CuCl2 (Table S1). These results showed that ChCl/FeCl3 pretreatment was more violent in deconstructing lignocellulose and promoting carbohydrate degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Property and photodegradation performance of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The recovered DES filtrate contained a large amount of solubilized degraded compounds such as xylose, lactic acid, and formic acid, which can directly act as solvents, dopants, and carbon precursors for the synthesis of carbon materials. Herein, the recovered DES filtrate was one-pot converted to N-doped CDs through a typical solvothermal method. Among three DES filtrates, the CDs from the ChCl/CuCl2 filtrate showed weak fluorescence intensity; hence, it has not been studied further. As a control, pure ChCl and pure ChCl/FeCl3 were directly used as carbon resources to prepare Ch-CDs and pure Fe-CDs, respectively. Under 365 nm UV light irradiation, all CDs emitted blue fluorescence, thereby mostly acting as a fluorescence probe (Fig. S1a) [21,22]. The quantum yields from ChCl/FeCl3 and pure ChCl/FeCl3 pretreated filtrate were higher than from pure ChCl and ChCl/AlCl3 pretreated filtrate (Fig. S2), suggesting the superiority of ChCl/FeCl3 in the preparation of CDs.

The UV–Vis spectra of all CDs presented a peak near 264–270 nm, which was attributed to the π–π* transition of conjugated CC (Fig. S1a) [23]. 3D fluorescence spectra showed that the optimal excitation wavelengths of all CDs were at around 350–375 nm, and the corresponding emission wavelengths were in the range of 420–465 nm (Fig. S1b). The emission centers and fluorescence intensity of four CDs presented a slight difference, which could be related to the carbon core structure [24]. 2D fluorescence spectra showed that the emission wavelengths of all CDs occurred redshift with the increasing of the excitation wavelength, suggesting the excitation-dependent emission phenomenon of CDs (Fig. S1c). This behavior could be due to inhomogeneous size distribution and diverse surface defects (oxygen and nitrogen-doped groups) [16].

The morphology and structure of CDs significantly affect their performance in optical and photodegradation. As illustrated in Fig. 2a, these CDs are a quasi-spherical carbon with a narrow size distribution from 0.6 to 4.0 nm. It has been reported that the particle size increased as the fluorescence emission redshifted [24]. The average particle size of the Fe-CDs (2.15 nm) was the largest, followed by Ch-CDs (2.13 nm), Al-CDs (2.01 nm), and pure Fe-CDs (1.54 nm), indicating that quantum diameter affected the emission wavelength. The lattice stripe spacing of all CDs was the same at 0.21 nm, similar to the lattice structure of graphited carbons, suggesting the existence of a highly crystalline graphitic structure.

Subsequently, XPS was used to investigate the surface elemental content and states of CDs. XPS full spectrum of CDs proved the existence of carbon (C), oxygen (O), and nitrogen (N) (Fig. S3a). The surface carbon atomic content of Fe-CDs was the highest, followed by Al-CDs, pure Fe-CDs, and Ch-CDs, implying the highest graphitized degree of Fe-CDs, thus possibly possessing the best optical performance (Fig. S3b). The C1s spectra of CDs showed three main peaks at 284.8 (C-C/C=C), 286.2 (CO), and 288.3 eV (C=O) (Fig. S4) [25]. The three-component N1s peaks at 399.1, 401.6, and 402.5 eV arose from pyridinic N, graphitic N, and ON, respectively (Fig. 2b) [26]. These nitrogen species deemed as active sites could alter the positive charges of the adjacent carbon atoms, thus promoting the adsorption and activation of PDS [25]. Besides, the O1s spectra also have three component peaks at 531.2 (C=O), 532.2 (CO), and 535.2 eV (ON) (Fig. S4) [26]. Among four CDs, the amount of CO in Fe-CDs was the highest, indicating that Fe-CDs were more prone to perform red-shifting emission, consistent with the result of fluorescence spectra (Fig. S5b). Overall, the introduction of N/O-containing functional groups and graphitic N atoms into the CDs was conducive to electron transfer and PDS activation, thereby enhancing photodegradation efficiency [25].

As a recently popular advanced oxidation technology, photocatalytic degradation possesses many advantages, such as broad-spectrum activity, low energy consumption, and minimal secondary pollution, showing promising applications in wastewater treatment [27]. Herein, the CDs with surface defects were applied to perform the photocatalytic degradation for MO solution. Electrochemical impedance spectra showed that the semicircular diameter of Fe-CDs was smaller than that of Al-CDs (Fig. 3a). This showed that Fe-CDs had lower charge transfer resistance, which guaranteed the effective transport and separation of charge carriers. Photocurrent response curves reflected the ability of photocatalysts to excite electrons and holes [28]. Fig. 3b showed that only Fe-CDs exhibited a clear photocurrent signal, and the photocurrent magnitude was unchanged, indicating the production of the photogenerated electrons and holes under light irradiation. UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectrum displayed the wide absorption range of Fe-CDs, close to the near-infrared region (Fig. 3c). The results revealed that Fe-CDs promoted the excitation of photogenerated electrons and separation and transfer of photogenerated carrier, thus enhancing photocatalytic ability.

Subsequently, the photocatalytic activity and mechanism of Fe-CDs using PDS as an oxidant were investigated for the degradation of MO dye. Before irradiation, the photocatalytic solutions were placed in the dark for 30 min until reaching the adsorption equilibrium, which eliminated the interference of the adsorption of the MO dye on the catalyst surface (Fig. S6). The Fe-CDs/PDS system exhibited better photocatalytic efficiency for the MO dye compared with the pure PDS, which was attributed to the effective interaction between PDS and Fe-CDs (Fig. 3d). The dopant atoms and surface defects of Fe-CDs facilitated the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), thus promoting the photodegradation process of MO dye. Additionally, the dye concentrations and initial pH in the solution largely affected the photocatalytic ability of Fe-CDs. The photocatalytic removal rate of MO dye gradually decreased with an increase in MO dye concentration, and the MO removal rate reached about 59 % at 50 mg·L−1 (Fig. 3e). The effect of initial pH on the MO degradation by Fe-CDs/PDS under light irradiation was investigated (Fig. 3f). The MO degradation efficiency significantly increased at lower pH values and reached 97 % under acidic conditions, indicating the generation of more reactive species in the acidic condition to promote the MO degradation [29].

To reveal the MO degradation mechanism of the Fe-CDs/PDS, radical quenching tests were conducted to disclose the contribution of individual ROS to photocatalytic reaction. Fig. 3g shows the MO removal rate with the presence of the specific scavengers, including Ar for •O2 −, TBA for •OH, EtOH for •OH and SO4 •−, and L-His for 1O2. The presence of EtOH, TBA, and Ar slightly inhibited the degradation of MO dye, while the addition of L-histidine resulted in the lowest MO removal rate. The result revealed that free radicals (•O2 −, •OH, SO4 •−) and non-radical singlet oxygen (1O2) were responsible for the degradation of the MO dye during Fe-CDs photoinduced PDS activation, in which 1O2 played a dominating role in the photodegradation process. Based on the Kubelka-Munk formula, the bandgap of Fe-CDs was 2.37 eV (Fig. S7). The valence band energy of Fe-CDs was higher than that of H+/H2, while the conduction band was more negative than that of H2O/O2, which was beneficial for the generation of •OH and •O2 − (Fig. 3h).

A plausible mechanism of the Fe-CDs-induced photocatalytic MO degradation by PDS activation is shown in Fig. 3i. The XPS curve showed that the presence of iron element in Fe-CDs was the form of FeN, which played a crucial role in collecting visible light by the light-induced metal charge transition (LMCT) and acting as active sites to accelerate electron transfer, thus promoting the photo-Fenton process (Fig. S4c) [21]. Moreover, the Fe-CDs with graphitic structure and N, O co-existing defects could isolate electron-hole pairs by capturing visible light. These photoinduced electrons could transfer the Fe(III) to Fe(II) via the LMCT process. During the photocatalytic process, the Fe(II) could activate PDS to produce SO4 •− which further reacted with H2O to generate •OH [30]. Meanwhile, the photogenerated electrons also could convert O2 to •O2 − which was converted into 1O2 with the aid of H2O [31]. During the process of transforming O2 to •O2 −, the Fe(II) was oxidized to the Fe(III). These reactive oxygen species resulted in the degradation of dye molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization and adsorption ability of Fe-doped porous carbon
ce:section.ce:para: Metal-based DES could be used as heteroatomic dopants and structural directing agents, exhibiting the enormous potential for controlling the preparation of heteroatom-doped porous carbons [14]. Lignin, containing a high carbon content and abundant phenolic hydroxyl groups, is a promising carbon resource. Given the plentiful lignin fragments and the leftover DES, the recovered residue from the ChCl/FeCl3 DES pretreatment was used as a carbon resource for preparing the Fe/N doped porous carbons to adsorb various dyes, achieving “treating pollutant with waste”. The well-defined structure of the Fe/N doped porous carbons was investigated to seize insights into why this carbon can considerably adsorb dyes. As observed in Fig. S8, a large amount of O, N, and Fe atoms were uniformly distributed in the ChCl/FeCl3 residue (R), confirming the presence of the leftover DES in the residue. This suggested that the residue is an excellent carbon resource for preparing the heteroatom-doped carbon materials. After carbonization, O, N, and Fe atoms in the residue remained in the porous activated carbons (AC), which generated more active sites to adsorb dyes (Fig. 4a).

XPS spectra of the R and AC samples also proved the successful doping of O, N, and Fe atoms (Figs. 4b–e and S9). The difference in the O1s spectra between the R and AC samples was that the AC samples had one more peak at 530.2 eV (OFe) (Fig. 4c) [21]. In the N1s spectra, the AC sample showed a sharp peak at 399.9 eV (pyrrolic N), while pyridinic N and oxidized N were observed in the R sample (Fig. 4d) [32]. The presence of O/N-containing functional groups on the AC can improve its surface hydrophilicity and form more hydrogen bonds with dye molecules, hence promoting its contact with dyes in water and enhancing its adsorption capacity. In the Fe 2p XPS spectrum, the R sample only observed one peak at 713.1 eV (Fe3+), while five peaks in the AC sample were identified at 710.9, 712.9, 718.8, 724.9, and 731.3 eV, corresponding to Fe2+ 2p2/3, Fe3+ 2p2/3, OFe, Fe2+ 2p1/2, Fe3+ 2p1/2, respectively (Fig. 4e) [16]. These iron groups can be combined with the negatively charged part of the dye molecule through electrostatic interaction to achieve the efficient adsorption of the dye. Compared with the R sample, the AC sample possessed a higher specific surface area (1818.2 cm2·g−1) and abundant porosity, producing more adsorption sites and thus promoting dye adsorption process (Fig. 4f). Raman spectrum indicated that the AC contained the graphitized structure, which can bind with the π electron donor groups in the dye molecules (aromatic ring structures) through the π-π interaction, thereby adsorbing dyes (Fig. 4g).

The adsorption capacity of the AC to Congo red (CR) dye at different dye concentrations is shown in Fig. 5a. As the CR concentration increased, the adsorption amount of the AC to the CR dye initially surged and slowly reached equilibrium at 200 mg·L−1. Data fitting using Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models revealed that the adsorption of CR on the AC was more in line with the Langmuir isotherm model (Fig. 5b), suggesting that the adsorption process was monolayer adsorption. The effect of contact time between the AC and CR dye on adsorption performance was discussed. As shown in Fig. 5c, the adsorption amount of the CR dye on the AC increased rapidly in the first 17 h, and the adsorption capacity gradually stabilized as time extended. The longer equilibrium time was attributed to the high specific surface area and abundant pore structures of the AC, making the dye molecules need a longer time to diffuse into the pores from the exterior and subsequently adsorb on the active sites on the internal surface [33]. The kinetic model results showed that the correlation coefficients (R2) of the pseudo-first-order model and pseudo-second-order model were 0.9524 and 0.9919 (Fig. 5c), respectively, both displaying perfect fitting results. This implied that the physical and chemical adsorption occurred simultaneously during the adsorption process.

Subsequently, the effect of the dye types on the adsorption capacity of the AC was also explored. As observed in Fig. 5d, the AC showed more superior adsorption capacity on the anionic dyes than on cationic dyes. This was mainly attributed to the abundant iron groups on the AC, which initiated the surface complexation and chemical adsorption with anionic dyes, thereby resulting in the high adsorption capacity [34]. The reuse of the AC on the adsorption of the CR dye was investigated using hydrochloric acid as a desorption agent. The adsorption capacity of the AC to the CR dye reached 2432.3 mg·g−1 at 200 mg·L−1, which outperformed most carbon materials that were synthesized by complex procedures and the use of special carbon precursors (Fig. 5a and g). Moreover, this AC offered simplicity in operation, high resource utilization, and less pollution. Recyclability experiments showed that the adsorption efficiency of the AC towards CR reduced as the number of cycles increased, where the CR removal rate of the AC remained at 828.7 mg·g−1 after the fourth cycles, still superior to some reported carbons (Fig. 5e) [35–37]. The phenomenon could be attributed to the strong interaction between the AC adsorbent and the uptake CR, which could cause irreversible adsorption of the CR dye [38]. Compared to other carbon-based dye adsorbents involved in complex procedures and the use of special carbon precursors, the AC adsorbent in this study involved excellent dye adsorbability, sustainability of raw materials, modest cycle efficiency, convenience, and economies of prepared procedure (Fig. 5f). Based on above experiment data, the superb adsorption capacity of the AC on the CR dye was attributed to the physical adsorption, coordination with the iron atoms, hydrogen bonding, electrostatic and π-π interactions (Fig. 5h).

A novel dual valorization strategy with DES-driven lignocellulose deconstruction, the efficient utilization of DES-derived waste and carbon materials' preparation was proposed for the efficient removal of dyes in the effluents. The ChCl/FeCl3 was employed to deconstruct lignocellulose and generate highly condensed lignin for the preparation of CDs and porous carbons. The CDs exhibited abundant nitrogen/oxygen functional groups, low charge transfer resistance, and strong photocurrent response, which resulted in a higher degradation efficiency of methyl orange (MO) than pure PDS. The acidic condition largely facilitated the MO degradation efficiency (97 %), with free radical quenching experiments indicating that 1O2 is the dominant oxidant species involved in the photocatalytic process of MO dye. The Fe-doped porous carbons showed a high specific surface area, abundant iron functional groups, and high graphitized carbon, thus displaying superior adsorption performance (2432.3 mg·g−1) and modest recyclability for Congo red, which outperformed most reported carbon materials. The efficient utilization of lignocellulose and the strategic employment of DES waste liquids in the production of functional carbon materials for environmental remediation not only drive the sustainable development of biorefineries but also embody the ‘waste-treats-pollutant’ concept, thereby advancing the implementation of sustainable and green chemical processes.

Si Hong: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Aocheng Wei: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Formal analysis. Chao Xie: Validation, Resources, Investigation. Xiaojun Shen: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Jia-Long Wen: Validation, Supervision, Resources. Tong-Qi Yuan: Validation, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (200-BLX202234), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31971613 and 22308029), and the 5·5 Engineering Research & Innovation Team Project of Beijing Forestry University (No. BLRC 2023B05). Additionally, the authors appreciate the assistance of the Innovation Plat-form for High-Value Utilization of Forest Resources at Beijing Forestry University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2024.137159.
